rulings.Case Title: PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA VS. PROPERTY SUBJECT TO DISPOSITION: $860.OO U.S. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answers To Petitioners First Set Of Demand For Production And Inspection Of Documents; Petitioners First Set Of Special Interrogatories; Judicial Council Form Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, May 17, 2018, Line 1, PETITIONER PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA's Motion To Compel Answers To Petitioners First Set Of Demand For Production And Inspection Of Documents; Petitioners First Set Of Special Interrogatories; Judicial Council Form Interrogatories. Pro Tem Judge Dianne Peebles, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Petitioner's Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answers To Petitioners First Set Of Demand For Production And Inspection Of Documents; Petitioners First Set Of Special Interrogatories; Judicial Council Form Interrogatories having been duly considered and no opposition thereto the court finds good cause and the motion is hereby GRANTED. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to dpeebleslaw@comcast.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

The Petitioner's Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel CLAIMANT YUTTASAK SIMMA'S Answers To Petitioners First Set Of Demand For Production And Inspection Of Documents; Petitioners First Set Of Special Interrogatories; Judicial Council Form Interrogatories is hereby GRANTED. The Motion to Compel CLAIMANT ROXANNE CASTILLO'S Answers to Petitioners First Set Of Special Interrogatories is hereby GRANTED. rulings.Case Title: MARC HONG SO MAC ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendants Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 16, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendants Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment. Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment is denied. There is a triable dispute whether the tree roots constituted a dangerous condition of public property. Plaintiff Marc Mac submitted evidence that: 1) the City knew that people walk into tree basins; and 2) the City has regulations about tree basins which protect against tripping hazards. This evidence precludes a determination as a matter of law that exposed roots within a tree basin is not a condition of property that creates a substantial risk of a tripping injury when used with due care. Whether Mr. Mac was himself negligent on the day of the incident is not relevant to the issue whether the property was in a dangerous when used with due care. (Fredette v. City of Long Beach (1986) 187 Cal.App.3d 122, 13). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Mac is required to prepare a proposed order which complies with CCP 437c(g) and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: CAROLINE MOIA VS. WILLIAMS-SONOMA INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Williams-Sonoma Inc. Motion For Summary Judgment , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. *For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only* (Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 16, 2018, Line 3, Defendant Williams-Sonoma Inc.'s Motion For Summary Judgment, Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. *For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only* (Part 2 Of 2) Fifth cause of action for disclosure of private facts: Ms. Moia failed to present any evidence that WSI or any of its employees made a public disclosure of the fact that Ms. Moia is an Atheist. Ms. Moia testified that she did not know whether Ms. Johnson or Ms. Lim disclosed this information. (Moia Depo, 232:3-11.) Sixth cause of action for assault and battery: This claim is preempted by workers' compensation exclusivity because the alleged conduct of two co-workers, Ms. Garratt and Mr. Bundalian, occurred within the scope of their employment. The exception under Lab. Code 3601(a)(1) does not apply because Ms. Moia failed to produce any evidence that Ms. Garrett and Mr. Bundalian intended to injure her. Seventh and ninth causes of action for failure to prevent religious discrimination and harassment: There is a triable dispute whether Ms. Moia complained to her supervisors and they failed to take any action. Eleventh cause of action for retaliation: There is a triable dispute whether Ms. Klein terminated Ms. Moia's employment because Ms. Moia complained about co-workers' comments regarding her religious beliefs. Twelfth cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress: The conduct and comments attributable to WSY, while objectively offensive and uncivil, were not so outrageous and extreme to support an IIED claim. (Yurick v. Superior Court (1989) 209 Cal. App. 3d 1116, 1128). Request for punitive damages: Ms. Moia failed to present any evidence that a WSI managing agent committed any act of oppression, fraud, or malice. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for WSI is required to prepare a proposed order which complies with CCP 437c(g) and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.(end of part 2 of 2) =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: CAROLINE MOIA VS. WILLIAMS-SONOMA INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 16, 2018, Line 3, DEFENDANT WILLIAMS-SONOMA INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication (part 1 of 2) Defendant Williams-Sonoma, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternative motion for summary adjudication is: a) denied as to the first, third, seventh, ninth, eleventh, and thirteenth causes of action in the second amended complaint for religious discrimination and harassment, failure to prevent religious discrimination and harassment, retaliation, and wrongful termination; b) granted as to the second, fourth, fifth, sixth, eighth, tenth, and twelfth causes of action in the second amended complaint for racial discrimination and harassment, disclosure of private facts, assault and battery, failure to prevent racial discrimination and harassment, and intentional infliction of emotional distress; and c) granted as to request for punitive damages. First and thirteenth causes of action for religious discrimination and wrongful termination: There is a triable dispute whether Ms. Klein terminated Ms. Moia because of Ms. Moia's religious beliefs. Ms. Moia testified at her deposition that on November 29, 2015 Ms. Klein told her "this religious issue" was getting out of hand, that she did not know how to handle it, and Ms. Moia's employment at WSI had ended. (Moia Depo. 236:23- 245:15.) Second, fourth, eighth and tenth causes of action for racial discrimination and harassment and failure to prevent racial discrimination and harassment: Ms. Moia failed to exhaust her administrative remedies as to her race-based claims. The two pre-complaint inquiry forms she relies on are inadmissible. Ms. Moia's DFEH complaint states that she was subjected to discrimination, harassment and retaliation by WSI based on religion and fails to mention anything about race. Third cause of action for religious harassment: There are triable disputes whether the conduct directed towards and comments made to Ms. Moia were: a) unwelcomed and b) severe or pervasive to create a hostile work environment. Ms. Moia testified that she disliked that Ms. Garrett and Mr. Bundalian consistently made comments, verbally attacked her, shoved her and aggressively snatched a clipboard and merchandise from her hands. While offhanded comments and isolated, infrequent incidents are not actionable harassment, Ms. Moia testified that the comments made were directed to her and happened frequently during the weeks leading up to her termination. (end of Part 1 of 2)

rulings.Case Title: MARK MAKOWIECKI VS. PACIFIC GAS & ELECTRIC COMPANY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 16, 2018, Line 5, DEFENDANTS PG&E CORPORATION, PACIFIC GAS & ELECTRIC COMPANY'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. (Part 1 of 2) Defendants PG&E Corporation and Pacific Gas & Electric Company, Inc.'s demurrer to all thirteen causes of action in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Mark Makowiecki is: a) sustained without leave to amend as to the first, second, third, eleventh and twelfth causes of action for breach of the implied contract of continued employment, breach of implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in an implied contract of employment, wrongful discharge in violation of public policy, breach of written contract for neutral reference, and breach of implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in the neutral reference contract; b) overruled as to the fourth, sixth, eighth, ninth, tenth and thirteenth causes of action for slander per se, intentional infliction of emotional distress, intentional interference with contact and economic advantage, negligent interference with contract and economic advantage, violation of the Unfair Competition Law, and injunctive relief; and c) sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to the fifth and seventh causes of action for invasion of privacy and negligent infliction of emotional distress, and overruled as to all other causes of action. First, second and third causes of action: These claims are preempted by the National Labor Relations Act because discussing wages among workers is an arguably protected activity and the NLRB examined questions of law and fact substantially identical to those presented by these claims and dismissed those claims/charges on the merits. (Parker v. Connors Steel Co. (11th Cir. 1988) 855 F.2d 1510, 1517 ("We believe that the primary jurisdiction rationale has the greatest validity when a party has sought redress for his claims from the NLRB and in the face of an adverse decision the claims are restructured as state law claims and pursued in state court.")). Fourth cause of action of action: Mr. Makowiecki has sufficiently alleged the substance of the slanderous statements and has alleged that they are false. Fifth cause of action: Mr. Makowiecki's allegations are insufficient to state a claim for invasion of privacy. If he can do so in good faith, he is given leave to allege facts that PG&E has "penetrated some zone of physical or sensory privacy ... or obtained unwanted access to data by electronic or other covert means, in violation of the law or social norms." (Hernandez v. Hillsides, Inc. (2009) 47 Cal.4th 272, 286.) (end part 1 of 2) =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: MARK MAKOWIECKI VS. PACIFIC GAS & ELECTRIC COMPANY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Pg&E Corporation, Pacific Gas & Electric Company Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 16, 2018, Line 5, Defendants PG&E Corporation, Pacific Gas & Electric Company Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2) Sixth cause of action: Mr. Makowiecki has adequately alleged extreme and outrageous conduct by PG&E by attempting to prevent Mr. Makowiecki from being employable in his chosen field. Seventh cause of action: Mr. Makowiecki's claim for negligent infliction of emotional distress fails to allege any independent duty by PG&E to avoid infliction of emotional distress. If he can do so in good faith, Mr. Makowiecki is given leave to correct this deficiency. Eighth and ninth causes of action: Mr. Makowiecki has sufficiently alleged intentional and negligent interference with contract and prospective economic advantage. Tenth and Thirteenth causes of action: The facts alleged to support the slander and interference claims also support the UCL claim and serve as a basis for injunctive relief. Eleventh and twelfth causes of action: The Butte Superior Court's order retaining jurisdiction is broad and can reasonably be interpreted to encompass the neutral reference claims. Comity and judicial economy counsel in favor of the Butte Court, not this court, interpreting the order and determining whether the Butte court has retained jurisdiction over these claims. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for PG&E is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.(end of part 2 of 2) =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: **TRANSFERRED TO SANTA CLARA ** TONI YOUNG VS. MICHAEL E. ADAMS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Change Venue
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 16, 2018, Line 6, DEFENDANT MICHAEL ADAMS Motion To Change Venue. Defendant Michael Adams' motion to change venue to Santa Clara County is granted. Mr. Adams' motion is not untimely since it was filed within the time he was granted to respond to the complaint. The contract venue provision providing for venue in the county where the obligation was to be performed is inapplicable because that provision is only applicable where there is a writing providing where the contract is to be performed and may not be shown by parol evidence. (Weil and Brown book at 3:503-504). Plaintiff Toni Young must pay all transfer costs. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Mr. Adams is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: SAMSON LAI ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 16, 2018, Line 7, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. (part 1 of 2) Defendant City and County of San Francisco's anti-SLAPP motion to strike specified language in paragraphs 21, 27, 28, 30, 34, and 38 of the complaint filed by Samson Lai et al is denied in its entirety. CCSF fails to satisfy its first prong burden of showing that any of the claims asserted by plaintiffs arise from activity protected by the anti-SLAPP statute. (Park v. Board of Trustees of Calif. State University (2017) 2 Cal.5th 1057.) In Park, a plaintiff professor brought an action for discrimination in denial of tenure against the defendant university. The California Supreme Court held that the university failed to carry its burden of showing that the denial of tenure arose from protected activity in CCP 425.16. The Court explained that a claim is not subject to an anti-SLAPP motion "simply because it contests an action or decision that was arrived at following speech or petitioning activity, or that was thereafter communicated by means of speech or petitioning activity. Rather, a claim may be struck only if the speech or petitioning activity itself is the wrong complained of, and not just evidence of liability or a step leading to some different act for which liability is asserted." (Park, 2 Cal.5th 1060 (italics in original).) Here, plaintiffs allege that Romero's relationship with Montes created a hostile work environment, Montes made false claims regarding harassment and discrimination at Station 2, and CCSF conducted a biased investigation that ultimately led to adverse employment actions (e.g. the transfer to other stations). CCSF fails to satisfy its first prong burden because CCSF's alleged biased investigation itself is not the wrong complained of. Rather, what gives rise to liability is that CCSF allegedly denied employment benefits. Park approvingly cited Nam v. Regents of University of California, (2016) 1 Cal.App.5th 1176. In Nam, a medical resident sued a university for sexual harassment, discrimination, and wrongful termination. The defendant's anti-SLAPP motion "contended the suit arose from communicated complaints about the plaintiff's performance, written warnings it issued her, an investigation it conducted, and the written notice to plaintiff of her termination." (Park, 2 Cal.5th at 1066.) (End of Part 1 of 2)

rulings.Case Title: SAMSON LAI ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL

rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 16, 2018, Line 7, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S Motion To Seal Records Lodged By Defendant In Support Of Special Motion To Strike. Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion to seal records lodged in support of its anti-SLAPP motion to strike is granted. No opposition filed and good cause shown. There is an overriding interest in sealing the cited documents to protect confidentiality and integrity of the investigatory process and the privacy rights of third parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the court will sign the proposed order submitted by CCSF. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: SAMSON LAI ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant City And County Of San Francisco Motion To Strike Complaint (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only) Part 1 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Defendant City And County Of San Francisco Motion To Strike Complaint (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only) (Part 2 Of 2). Nam held that the basis for the plaintiff's claims was not speech itself, but rather the university's alleged conduct. "What gives rise to liability is not that the defendant spoke, but that the defendant denied the plaintiff a benefit ... on account of a discriminatory or retaliatory consideration." (Id.) The same is true in this case. At most, Montes' complaint to CCSF and CCSF's conduct during the ensuing investigation are merely steps leading up to the adverse employment decisions taken against plaintiffs. (See Park, 2 Cal.5th at 1060.) While CCSF's purportedly biased investigation may supply evidence of animus, it does not convert the investigation itself into the basis for liability. (See Park, 2 Cal.5th at 1068.) The analysis would be different if, for example, the plaintiffs sued for defamation based on CCSF's publishing false charges during the course of the investigation. CCSF's citations to case law for the proposition that all communications relating to and decisions arising out of an internal sexual harassment investigation are protected under the anti-SLAPP statute either predate Park or are distinguishable. For example, CCSF's reliance on Okorie v. Los Angeles Unified School District, (2017) 14 Cal.App.5th 574 is unavailing. There, the court specifically found that "in contrast to Park, the protected activity here itself is the wrong complained of." (Id. at 592.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and a proposed judgment which conforms to the tentative ruling and must bring them to the hearing or email them to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of part 2 of 2) =(302/HEK)
